revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Gion cult]][[ja:祇園信仰]][[simple:Gion worship]]
{{Short description|Cult of Gozu Tenno and Susanoo}} [[File:Yasaka_Shrine_八坂神社_【Gaia_Walker_Slide_Show_Demonstration】_-_panoramio_(1).jpg|thumb|[[Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka shrine]] during the [[Gion Matsuri|Gion festival]], the largest shrine and festival of the Gion faith]] {{nihongo|'''Gion worship'''|祇園信仰|Gion shinkō}} is a Shinto cult. Originally it revolved solely around [[Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tenno]], but during the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} the government mandated it shift to revolving around [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]].<ref name="gozu">川村『牛頭天王と蘇民将来伝説——消された異神たち』（2007）</ref>
The main shrines are [[Yasaka Shrine]] in Kyoto, [[Hiromine Shrine]] in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}},<ref name="kyotokankoyagi-2021a">{{Cite web |last=kyotokankoyagi |date=2021-01-06 |title=Gozu Tenno and Yasaka shrine: The Deity is still alive in the fear of COVID-19 牛頭天王英語で説明 |url=https://kyotokankoyagi.com/gozutenno-en |access-date=2022-06-16 |website=ヤギの京都観光案内/KYOTO GOAT BLOG |language=ja}}</ref> and [[Tsushima Shrine]] in {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|qid=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}}
There are many other cults of Susanoo that are not derived from the Gion faith, but rather from indigenous Shinto traditions without Buddhist influence.{{Citation needed|date=June 2022}} These include [[Susa Shrine]], and [[Yaegaki Shrine]].{{Citation needed|date=June 2022}}
== History == Gozu Tenno was originally a {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}-style [[Onmyōdō]] deity, and is generally considered to be the guardian deity of {{ill|Jetavana|en|Jetavana|qid=Q305242|lt=Jetavana|1=Jetavana}}, the monastery where [[:en:Gautama Buddha|the Buddha]] studied.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Lillehoj|first=Elizabeth|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FogSFWDe0hUC&pg=PA218|title=Critical Perspectives on Classicism in Japanese Painting: 1600 - 1700|date=2004-01-01|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-2699-4|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Japan Shinto Kami Gods {{!}} Gozu-Tennō 牛頭天王{{!}} Rods Shinto|url=https://www.rodsshinto.com/gozu-tenno|access-date=2022-01-10|website=shintoshrines|language=en}}</ref>
According to legend, from the 6th to 7th centuries CE, {{ill|Hōdō|en|Hōdō|qid=Q11555373|lt=Hōdō|1=Hōdō}} traveled from India through China and the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|qid=Q18097|lt=Korean|1=Korea}} kingdom of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and eventually arrived in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. He is recognized as the founder of temples in the mountains of {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|qid=Q1047523|lt=Harima Province|1=Harima Province}} and is associated with several ''chokugan-ji'' (勅願寺), Buddhist temples built at the request of the reigning emperor.<ref name="faure-2021a">{{Cite book |last=Faure |first=Bernard |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gYOUEAAAQBAJ&dq=H%C5%8Dd%C5%8D+gozu+tenno&pg=PA307 |title=Rage and Ravage: Gods of Medieval Japan, Volume 3 |date=2021-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-8936-4 |language=en}}</ref>
Tradition holds that when Hōdō came to Japan, he was accompanied by the deity [[Gozu Tennō]] (牛頭天王; {{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|qid=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}}: ''Gośīrṣa devarāja''), who was later enshrined at [[Hiromine Shrine]] and [[Yasaka Shrine]] and is venerated in the [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]].<ref name="faure-2021a" />
While engaged in ascetic practice on {{ill|Mount Rokkō|en|Mount Rokkō|qid=Q462120|lt=Mount Rokkō|1=Mount Rokkō}} at the [[Kumoga Iwa]] Rock (雲ヶ岩), Hōdō is said to have been approached by the deity {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|qid=Q866315|lt=Vaiśravaṇa|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} riding on purple clouds. It was after this encounter that Hōdō built Tamon-ji in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}} to enshrine the Kumoga Iwa Rock, {{ill|Rokkō-Hime-Daizen-no-Kami|1=Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine|2=ja|3=六甲比命神社|lt=Rokkō-Hime-Daizen-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkō-Hime-Daizen-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine|qq=}} (六甲比命大善神), and the [[Shinkyō Iwa]]Rock.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |year=2012 |title=法道 |encyclopedia=Nihon Jinmei Daijiten (日本人名大辞典) |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |language=Japanese |trans-title=Hōdō |accessdate=2012-10-03 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25}}</ref>
The description in {{ill|Shinnaiden|ja|三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinnaiden|qq=}} is prominent. In China, he was influenced by [[Taoism]], and in Japan, he further merged with Susanoo, the [[Kami]] of [[Shinto]]. This is because both Gozu Tenno and Susanoo were considered to be plague gods.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=McMullin|first=Neil|date=1988-02-01|title=On Placating the Gods and Pacifying the Populace: The Case of the Gion "Goryō" Cult|url=https://www.journals.uchicago.edu/doi/10.1086/463123|journal=History of Religions|volume=27|issue=3|pages=270–293|doi=10.1086/463123|s2cid=162357693|issn=0018-2710}}</ref> He was considered to be the Buddha {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Bhaisajyaguru|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}.<ref name="gozu" />
The cult began in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, and the original form of the Gion faith was to prevent epidemics by comforting the god of pestilence. In the late 10th century, the citizens of Kyoto began to hold a festival at [[Yasaka Shrine]] (then known as Gion Shrine) which became known as [[Gion Matsuri]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Chapin|first=Helen B.|date=September 1934|title=The Gion Shrine and the Gion Festival|url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/594168|journal=Journal of the American Oriental Society|volume=54|issue=3|pages=282–289|doi=10.2307/594168|jstor=594168|issn=0003-0279}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=The Gion Festival: Exploring Its Mysteries|url=https://www.gionfestival.org/book/|access-date=2022-01-10|website=The Gion Festival|language=en-US}}</ref> By the Mi11le Ages, the Gion faith had spread throughout the country, and Gion shrines or Gyototenno shrines were created to enshrine Gyotenno, and the Goryokai (or Tenno Festival) was held as a ritual procession.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=http://archive.org/details/bu11haskamijapan00teeu|title=Buddhas and kami in Japan [electronic resource] : honji suijaku as a combinatory paradigm|last2=Rambelli|first2=Fabio|date=2003|publisher=London ; New York : RoutledgeCurzon|others=Library Genesis|isbn=978-0-203-22025-2}}</ref>
In the Meiji era (1868-1912), the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] banned Buddhist rituals at shrines and prohibited the use of Buddhist words such as "Gozu Tenno" and "Gion" in the names of deities and company names, so Gion Shrine and Gozu Tenno Shrine became shrines dedicated to Susanoo and changed their names.<ref name="gozu" />
== Hiromine Gion Cult == {{Main|Hiromine Shrine}} At Hiromine Shrine there is a quite different Gion cult revolving around agriculture rather than protection from disease. Interestingly Susanoo/Gozu Tenno is often observed as more intimidating in this tradition taking on elements of a [[Landlord deity]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=FAURE |first=BERNARD |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1k3np30 |title=Rage and Ravage |date=2021-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-8936-4 |doi=10.2307/j.ctv1k3np30}}</ref>{{rp||pages=124-125}}
==Gion shrines== There are many Gion shrines, [[Yasaka Shrine]] being the most prominent.<ref name="kyotokankoyagi-2021a" /> Others include: * [[Yasaka Shrine]] (八坂神社) in [[Gion]], {{ill|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|en|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1203862|lt=Higashiyama|1=Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} – Head shrine of the Yasaka [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] * [[Tsushima Shrine]] (津島神社) in {{ill|Tsushima, Aichi|en|Tsushima, Aichi|qid=Q816736|lt=Tsushima|1=Tsushima, Aichi}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|qid=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}} – Head shrine of the Tsushima shrine network * [[Hiromine Shrine]] (広峰神社) in {{ill|Himeji|en|Himeji|qid=Q209801|lt=Himeji|1=Himeji}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, which has a very different Gion cult focused on agriculture * {{ill|Nanba Shrine|ja|難波神社|1=Nanba Shrine|de|Namba-Schrein|fr|Namba-jinja|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanba Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanba Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Osaka City|Osaka City]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *{{Commonscatinline|Gion cult}} *[https://www.gion-shinkou.com/ 祇園信仰研究会] - 有志による研究会
